title: Endocrine Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for hormones, glands, and feedback mechanisms

questions.q: Hormones that are derived from amino acids include:
questions.explain: Amino acid-derived hormones include thyroid hormones (from tyrosine), catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine), and others like melatonin.

optionExplains.text: Steroids are derived from cholesterol, not amino acids.
Prostaglandins are eicosanoids derived from arachidonic acid (fatty acid).
Thyroid hormones (T3, T4) and catecholamines derive from tyrosine. Melatonin and histamine derive from other amino acids.
Eicosanoids come from fatty acids, not amino acids.

questions.q: The anterior pituitary is also called the:
questions.explain: The adenohypophysis (glandular hypophysis) is the anterior pituitary — it develops from oral ectoderm and produces six major hormones.

optionExplains.text: Neurohypophysis is the posterior pituitary — neural tissue from hypothalamus.
Adenohypophysis — 'adeno' means gland. It produces GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, and prolactin, controlled by hypothalamic releasing hormones.
Hypothalamus controls the pituitary but is a separate structure.
Pineal gland produces melatonin — different structure entirely.

questions.q: Which hormone stimulates the thyroid gland to release T3 and T4?
questions.explain: Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) from the anterior pituitary stimulates thyroid follicular cells to produce and release T3 and T4.

optionExplains.text: ACTH stimulates the adrenal cortex, not thyroid.
TSH — released when TRH from hypothalamus reaches the pituitary. TSH binds thyroid receptors, stimulating iodine uptake and hormone synthesis.
FSH stimulates ovarian follicles and spermatogenesis.
LH triggers ovulation and testosterone production.

questions.q: The posterior pituitary releases:
questions.explain: The posterior pituitary stores and releases ADH (antidiuretic hormone) and oxytocin — made by hypothalamic neurons and transported down axons.

optionExplains.text: Growth hormone and prolactin are anterior pituitary products.
ADH and oxytocin — made in hypothalamic neurons (supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei), transported to posterior pituitary for storage and release.
TSH and ACTH are anterior pituitary hormones.
FSH and LH are gonadotropins from anterior pituitary.

questions.q: Insulin is produced by which cells of the pancreas?
questions.explain: Beta cells in pancreatic islets (islets of Langerhans) produce insulin, which lowers blood glucose by promoting cellular uptake.

optionExplains.text: Alpha cells produce glucagon — raises blood glucose.
Beta cells — they sense blood glucose levels and release insulin proportionally. Destruction of beta cells causes Type 1 diabetes.
Delta cells produce somatostatin — regulates other islet hormones.
Acinar cells produce digestive enzymes — exocrine, not endocrine.

questions.q: Steroid hormones are derived from:
questions.explain: All steroid hormones — cortisol, aldosterone, testosterone, estrogen, progesterone — are synthesized from cholesterol.

optionExplains.text: Amino acid-derived hormones include catecholamines and thyroid hormones.
Cholesterol — the four-ring steroid backbone is modified differently to create glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, androgens, estrogens, and progestins.
Glucose is metabolized for energy, not used to make hormones.
Nucleic acids encode proteins, not steroid hormones.

questions.q: The 'master gland' that controls many other endocrine glands is the:
questions.explain: The pituitary gland secretes tropic hormones that control thyroid, adrenal cortex, and gonads — earning its 'master gland' title.

optionExplains.text: Thyroid is controlled BY pituitary TSH.
Adrenal cortex is controlled BY pituitary ACTH.
Pituitary — its tropic hormones (TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH) regulate other glands. But the hypothalamus controls the pituitary, so some say hypothalamus is the true master.
Pineal produces melatonin but doesn't control other glands.

questions.q: Calcitonin is produced by:
questions.explain: Calcitonin is produced by parafollicular (C) cells of the thyroid. It lowers blood calcium by inhibiting osteoclasts.

optionExplains.text: Parathyroid glands produce PTH, which RAISES calcium — opposite of calcitonin.
Thyroid gland — specifically the C cells between follicles. Calcitonin is released when blood calcium is high, promoting calcium deposition in bone.
Adrenal glands produce cortisol, aldosterone, and catecholamines.
Pancreas produces insulin and glucagon for glucose regulation.

questions.q: Second messengers like cAMP are used by:
questions.explain: Protein and peptide hormones can't enter cells — they bind surface receptors and use second messengers (cAMP, IP3, calcium) to relay signals inside.

optionExplains.text: Steroid hormones enter cells directly and bind intracellular receptors.
Thyroid hormones also enter cells and bind nuclear receptors.
Protein/peptide hormones are water-soluble and can't cross membranes. G protein-coupled receptors activate adenylyl cyclase, producing cAMP as a second messenger.
Different hormone classes use different mechanisms.

questions.q: The adrenal medulla produces:
questions.explain: The adrenal medulla is modified sympathetic nervous tissue releasing catecholamines — about 80% epinephrine, 20% norepinephrine.

optionExplains.text: Cortisol and aldosterone come from adrenal CORTEX.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine — chromaffin cells of the medulla release these catecholamines during stress, amplifying sympathetic responses.
Testosterone comes from testes (and some from adrenal cortex).
Insulin comes from pancreatic beta cells.

questions.q: Negative feedback in the endocrine system means:
options: Rising hormone levels inhibit further release
questions.explain: Negative feedback maintains homeostasis — when hormone levels rise, they inhibit the releasing mechanisms, preventing overproduction.

optionExplains.text: Continuous increase would be positive feedback, not negative.
Rising levels inhibit release — thyroid hormones inhibit TRH and TSH; cortisol inhibits CRH and ACTH. The product turns off its own production.
Failure to respond would be pathology, not negative feedback.
Enhancing responses is positive feedback.

questions.q: The zona glomerulosa of the adrenal cortex produces:
questions.explain: The zona glomerulosa (outermost cortical layer) produces mineralocorticoids — primarily aldosterone, which regulates sodium and potassium balance.

optionExplains.text: Glucocorticoids (cortisol) come from zona fasciculata (middle layer).
Mineralocorticoids — aldosterone from the glomerulosa increases sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion in kidneys. Think: 'Glomerulosa = minerals.'
Androgens come from zona reticularis (innermost cortex).
Catecholamines come from adrenal medulla, not cortex.

questions.q: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) acts to:
questions.explain: PTH raises blood calcium by stimulating osteoclasts, increasing renal calcium reabsorption, and activating vitamin D.

optionExplains.text: Lowering calcium is calcitonin's job.
Raise blood calcium — PTH pulls calcium from bone (osteoclast activation), reduces calcium loss in urine, and activates vitamin D to increase intestinal calcium absorption.
Blood glucose is regulated by insulin and glucagon, not PTH.
PTH doesn't affect blood glucose.

questions.q: Growth hormone (GH) is also known as:
questions.explain: Somatotropin is another name for growth hormone — 'somato' refers to body, 'tropin' refers to stimulating growth.

optionExplains.text: Prolactin stimulates milk production — different hormone.
Somatotropin — GH promotes tissue growth, especially bone and muscle. It works partly through IGF-1 (insulin-like growth factor) produced by the liver.
Thyroxine (T4) is thyroid hormone — metabolic regulation.
Corticotropin is ACTH — stimulates adrenal cortex.

questions.q: Glucagon is released in response to:
questions.explain: Glucagon from pancreatic alpha cells is released when blood glucose drops. It promotes glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to raise glucose.

optionExplains.text: High glucose triggers insulin release, not glucagon.
Low blood glucose — glucagon mobilizes glucose stores. It tells the liver to break down glycogen and make new glucose, preventing hypoglycemia.
Blood calcium is regulated by PTH and calcitonin, not glucagon.
Blood oxygen doesn't directly regulate glucagon release.

questions.q: The thyroid gland is located:
options: In the neck, anterior to the trachea
questions.explain: The thyroid gland is a butterfly-shaped gland in the anterior neck, wrapping around the trachea below the larynx.

optionExplains.text: Pituitary and pineal are in the brain, not thyroid.
Neck, anterior to trachea — two lobes connected by an isthmus. It moves with swallowing because it's attached to the trachea.
Above the kidneys describes adrenal gland location.
Pelvic location describes reproductive glands (ovaries/testes).

questions.q: Tropic hormones:
options: Are only produced by the thyroid
questions.explain: Tropic hormones target other endocrine glands. TSH stimulates thyroid, ACTH stimulates adrenal cortex, FSH and LH stimulate gonads.

optionExplains.text: Tropic hormones specifically target endocrine glands.
Stimulate other endocrine glands — 'tropic' means turning toward or stimulating. These hormones control the activity of target glands.
Tropic hormones are produced by the pituitary, not thyroid.
Tropic hormones are heavily regulated by negative feedback.

questions.q: Melatonin is produced by the:
questions.explain: The pineal gland produces melatonin in response to darkness, regulating circadian rhythms and sleep-wake cycles.

optionExplains.text: Pituitary produces many hormones but not melatonin.
Pineal gland — this small gland in the brain produces melatonin at night. Light inhibits melatonin synthesis; darkness promotes it.
Thymus produces thymic hormones for T cell development.
Parathyroid glands produce PTH for calcium regulation.

questions.q: Cortisol is classified as a:
questions.explain: Cortisol is the primary glucocorticoid — it affects glucose metabolism, suppresses inflammation, and helps manage stress.

optionExplains.text: Mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) regulate minerals — sodium and potassium.
Glucocorticoid — cortisol promotes gluconeogenesis, has anti-inflammatory effects, and helps the body cope with stress. It's the 'stress hormone.'
Catecholamines are epinephrine and norepinephrine from adrenal medulla.
Cortisol is a steroid hormone, not peptide.

questions.q: The hypothalamus controls the anterior pituitary via:
questions.explain: Hypothalamic releasing and inhibiting hormones travel via the hypophyseal portal system to control anterior pituitary hormone secretion.

optionExplains.text: Direct neural connections control the posterior pituitary, not anterior.
Releasing and inhibiting hormones — GHRH, TRH, CRH, GnRH stimulate release; dopamine and somatostatin inhibit. The portal system delivers them directly.
Posterior pituitary is controlled separately by neural connections.
Calcium levels don't directly control hypothalamus-pituitary axis.

questions.q: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) promotes:
questions.explain: ADH (vasopressin) increases water reabsorption in kidney collecting ducts, concentrating urine and conserving body water.

optionExplains.text: ADH does the opposite — it reduces water loss.

Water reabsorption — ADH inserts aquaporin channels in collecting duct cells. Water flows back into blood, producing concentrated urine. Without ADH, you'd produce liters of dilute urine (diabetes insipidus).
Sodium handling is primarily aldosterone's role.
Blood glucose is insulin/glucagon territory.

questions.q: The islets of Langerhans are located in the:
questions.explain: Islets of Langerhans are clusters of endocrine cells scattered throughout the pancreas, producing insulin, glucagon, and somatostatin.

optionExplains.text: Liver responds to pancreatic hormones but doesn't contain islets.
Pancreas — about 1-2 million islets dot the pancreas, comprising only 1-2% of its mass. The rest is exocrine (digestive enzyme production).
Thyroid has follicles, not islets.
Adrenal gland has cortex and medulla organization, not islets.

questions.q: Prolactin primarily stimulates:
questions.explain: Prolactin from the anterior pituitary stimulates mammary gland development and milk production after childbirth.

optionExplains.text: Ovulation is triggered by LH surge.
Milk production — prolactin levels rise during pregnancy and lactation. Suckling stimulates more prolactin release, maintaining milk production.
Sperm production is stimulated by FSH.
Thyroid hormone release is stimulated by TSH.

questions.q: Up-regulation of hormone receptors occurs when:
questions.explain: Up-regulation increases receptor numbers when hormone levels are low — making target cells more sensitive to the limited hormone available.

optionExplains.text: Chronically high levels cause down-regulation — fewer receptors.
Chronically low hormone levels — cells compensate by making more receptors. This increases sensitivity. Example: Diabetics may up-regulate insulin receptors.
Gland removal would reduce hormone, potentially causing up-regulation, but isn't the direct cause.
Feedback elimination doesn't directly cause receptor changes.

questions.q: Oxytocin stimulates:
questions.explain: Oxytocin causes uterine smooth muscle contraction during labor and myoepithelial cell contraction for milk let-down reflex.

optionExplains.text: Water retention is ADH's role.
Uterine contractions and milk ejection — oxytocin's dual roles. During labor, it drives contractions (positive feedback loop). During nursing, it causes milk let-down.
Bone growth is influenced by GH, thyroid hormones, and sex steroids.
Fat metabolism involves cortisol, thyroid hormones, and growth hormone.
